segments.s105-g1: This is a nominal phrase: سپهدار ترکان is a construct meaning 'the commander (سپهدار) of the Turks (ترکان)'. دو دیده پرآب is an appositive description 'two eyes (دیده) full of water (پرآب)', i.e. tearful; Persian places the modifying adjective phrase after the noun (دیده پرآب).
segments.s105-g2: شگفتی (astonishment) is the noun-subject that pairs with the compound verb فرو ماند (فرو + ماند) — here meaning 'was cast down/was overwhelmed' or 'was struck with'. ز introduces the causal/agentive source ('by/because of') and names افراسیاب as the cause. The clause as a whole is an intransitive past describing the emotional effect on the previously mentioned commander.
segments.s105-g3: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain'; مرد با هوش is a noun modified by a prepositional attributive phrase ('a man with intellect' = 'a clever man'). را is the definite/direct-object marker showing that this clause's verb برگزید (past 3rd sg) takes 'a certain clever man' as its object.
segments.s105-g4: فرسته is a noun 'envoy/messenger' placed after the object as an appositional role (he made him an envoy). به ایران marks destination 'to Iran'. چنان چون is an idiomatic pair meaning 'in such a way / as', and سزید (past of سزیدن used impersonally) means 'it was fitting/appropriate' or 'he deserved so' — here expressing that the sending was fitting.
segments.s105-g5: یکی again means 'a certain'; نامه is the direct object 'letter'. بنوشت is the archaic past-tense verb 'he wrote' (verb-final Persian order: subject/object before verb). ارتنگ‌وار is a postpositive adjective/adverbial phrase 'in the Artang manner' (وار = '-like').
segments.s105-g6: برو کرده is a compact expression meaning roughly 'having done upon it' (here: 'having applied to it'); کرد/کرده is the perfect/past participle 'done/made'. صد گونه is a numeral phrase 'a hundred kinds/types' modifying رنگ و نگار ('colors and ornaments/designs'). The whole fragment describes how the letter was embellished.
segments.s105-g7: به نام is the fixed formula 'in the name of' (به + noun). خداوند خورشید و ماه is a title phrase 'Lord of the Sun and the Moon' with خورشید و ماه as coordinated modifiers; Persian places the possessor/ title before its modifiers here.
segments.s105-g8: که is a relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing a clause about God. او is the pronoun 'he'; داد is the past-tense verb 'gave'. بر is the preposition 'upon/on'; آفرین here relates to creation/creating (derived from the root 'آفریدن'); دستگاه is 'order/arrangement' or 'system'. Together: 'who gave order upon creation.'
segments.s105-g65: This clause uses an existential/possessive negation: مرا نیست literally 'to me there is not', idiomatically 'I do not have' or 'I am free of'. The structure places the experiencer (مرا) before the negative copula (نیست), and the prepositional phrase از کینه و آز (literally 'from hatred and greed') states the source/reason. رنج at the end names the kind of thing (suffering) that would arise from those sources.
segments.s105-g66: بسیچیده‌ام is a poetic perfect first‑person form meaning 'I have gathered/experienced much' (here idiomatically 'I have endured much'). در marks location 'in', followed by سرای (here 'house'/'domain' though the provided lemma senses don't match, so this is a poetic/place name use) and سپنج (a noun, literal 'sponge' used as a place-name or metaphorical place). The verb form -ام shows the speaker (I) as subject completing the thought begun in the previous clause.
segments.s105-g67: شما را is the accusative/topic-marked 'you' (pl./formal) with را. سپردم is the simple past 'I entrusted/placed' (first person singular). ازان 'from/that' refers back to something previously mentioned ('that [thing]'), and روی آب means 'on the surface of the water' (روی = 'on/upon', آب = 'water'). Word order is subject implied 'I' (in سپردم), object شما را, then the locational phrase.
segments.s105-g68: مگر here introduces a rhetorical question or doubt ('could it be that...?' / 'isn't it that...'). یابد is a present/subjunctive-type form meaning 'find/attain' (classical usage, third person singular). آرامش is the object 'peace/tranquility', and افراسیاب is the proper name. The clause therefore asks rhetorically whether Afrasiab might attain peace.
segments.s105-g69: بنوی is an imperative or hortatory 'write!' used here as a narrative device ('write/record that...'). یکی = 'one' used idiomatically as 'someone' (indefinite subject). باز means 'again'. پیمان نوشت is a verbal phrase 'wrote a pact/covenant' with نوشت as the simple past 'he wrote'. The clause strings an imperative instruction with a report of an action.
segments.s105-g70: به introduces the location 'in/into' (here 'in a garden'). باغ بزرگی is 'a great/large garden' where بزرگی functions adjectivally after باغ in classical word order. درختی is an indefinite object 'a tree' (the -ی ending marks indefiniteness), and بکشت is an archaic past form meaning 'he planted/caused to grow' (here the main verb for the action).
segments.s105-g71: فرستاده is the noun 'messenger' (subject). آمد is the simple past 'came'. بسان is a comparative particle 'like/as', and پلنگ is the simile noun 'leopard' — together they form the simile 'came like a leopard'. Word order is Subject–Verb–simile phrase, typical in narrative description.
segments.s105-g72: رسانید is the past tense 'he delivered/conveyed' (here transitive). نامه is the direct object 'letter'. به نزد is a locative/phrasing meaning 'to the presence of' or 'before', and پشنگ is the personal name of the addressee; the whole phrase means 'delivered the letter to Peshang'.
segments.s105-g33: ببخشیم is 1st-person-plural present-subjunctive/hortative used as an exhortation (“let us forgive”). و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” زان پس is a temporal/adverbial phrase (“from that, afterwards” — زان = “from that/thereon,” پس = “after”). نجوییم is the negative present-subjunctive/hortative of جستن used here with the same hortatory force as ببخشیم (“let us not seek/pursue”), and کین is its object meaning “vengeance/hatred.”
segments.s105-g34: که introduces the reason/explanation here (”for/because”). چندین is an indefinite numeral/adjective “many/several.” بلا is the noun “calamity, affliction.” خود functions as an intensifier/reflective “itself/own” here. نیرزد is a present-tense negative predicate meaning “is not worth / does not merit.” زمین is the noun “earth/ground.” The clause gives the reason why one should not pursue vengeance.
segments.s105-g35: سراینده is the subject “the poet/songster.” از سال is a causal/temporal phrase “from/of years / through age” (از here = “because of/through”). چون is the simile marker “like/as,” و برف is the comparison term “snow.” گشت is the simple past third-person singular of گشتن meaning “became/turned.” Together: “the poet, through the years, turned like snow.”
segments.s105-g36: ز is the poetic form of از meaning “from/through/because of.” خون کیان is a genitive construction “the blood of the Kians” (خون possibly implying bloodshed). خاک is the subject “the earth/soil.” شنگرف is a descriptive noun/adjective meaning a bright/reddish sheen (here describing the soil), and گشت (past) is “became.” The phrase means the earth took on a bright (blood‑stained) sheen because of the Kians' blood.
segments.s105-g37: سرانجام is an adverb meaning “in the end / finally.” هم is an emphatic particle here (“even/also”). جز is the exception particle “except.” به بالای خویش is a prepositional phrase: به = “to/for,” بالای = “height/above/rank,” and خویش (here split into خوی + ش) = “one's own” (possessive). The phrase sets up the restriction: only what pertains to one’s own rank remains.
segments.s105-g38: نیابد is the negative present of یافتن used generically “does not find / does not obtain.” کسی here in a negative context means “no one.” بهره is the noun “share / benefit.” از indicates origin “from/of,” جای is “place/position,” and خویش + ش again marks the possessor “his own.” The clause says that no one obtains any share from his place except that which is due to his own rank.
segments.s105-g39: بمانیم is 1st-person-plural present-subjunctive/hortative used here as a statement about what will be (“we remain / we will remain”). روز پسین is a temporal phrase “the late/last day.” زیر خاک is a locative phrase “under the earth/under the soil.” The verb-first order gives a poetical, general assertion about the fate of people.
segments.s105-g40: سراپای (from سراپا) is an adverbial phrase “head to foot / wholly.” کرباس is the coarse cloth or sackcloth used as a burial shroud. و is “and.” جای is “place,” and مغاک is “abyss” (figuratively the grave). The phrase describes being entirely in sackcloth in the place of the abyss (the tomb).
segments.s105-g81: نبد is an archaic negative-existential form (≈ نبَود) meaning 'there was not / did not exist'. پیشتر is an adverb 'earlier/before'. آشتی را نشان is a noun phrase 'the sign/token of truce', with را marking it as a definite/direct object or topical element (classical poetic word order places the negation and adverb before the object here).
segments.s105-g82: بدین روز is the fused classical locative expression (به + این) meaning 'on/at this day' (the provided tokenization separates ب and دین; treat بدین as 'به + این'). گرز من is the agent/subject 'my mace' and آوردشان is the past verb 'brought them' (شان is the 3rd-person plural object clitic). The adverbial phrase modifies آوردشان.
segments.s105-g83: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'. گفت is the past-tense verb 'said/spoke' (here the lexical senses list is not giving the verb sense, so use the verbal reading). با before a personal name in classical usage can mark the agent ('by' or 'by the famed ...') — thus با نامور کیقباد = 'by the famed Kay Qobad' (i.e. 'the famed Kay Qobad said'). Word order places the verb before the agent for stylistic/poetic effect.
segments.s105-g84: که introduces a quoted/content clause 'that / that ...'. چیزی ندیدم is a negative past construction meaning 'I saw nothing / I have not seen anything' (ندیدم = ن + دیدم, past negation). نکوتر is the comparative form 'better' (نکو + تر). ز is the poetic preposition 'than/from' and داد here means 'justice' — so the clause says 'I have seen nothing better than justice.'
segments.s105-g85: نبیره فریدون is a genitive construct meaning 'the descendant/scion of Fereydun' (possessor follows possessed in classical Persian). فرخ پشنگ is an epithet/name (Farrokh Pashang) in apposition to the descendant; فرخ here is an epithet 'fortunate/auspicious' attached to the proper name پشنگ.
segments.s105-g86: به سیری is an adverbial phrase 'for a turn / for a while' (سیری here = a turn/interval). همی is the classical continuous/habitual marker that combines with a present-form verb to indicate ongoing or habitual action. سر بپیچد is a verbal phrase 'turns his head' (بپیچد is a present/subjunctive-form used idiomatically with همی to mean 'turns/averts'). ز جنگ = 'from battle/war' (ز = from).
segments.s105-g87: سزدگر is an impersonal/predicative form meaning 'it is fitting/appropriate'. هر آنکس که دارد خرد is a relative clause 'whoever/anyone who has wisdom' (هر آنکس = 'whoever'; که introduces the relative clause; دارد is the present 'has'; خرد = 'wisdom'). The whole phrase functions as the subject/holder of what is fitting.
segments.s105-g88: بکژی is an archaic verb-form here meaning 'to err / to act perversely' (the lemma list gives this as an irregular/obscure verb). The conjunction و = 'and' links two admonitions. ناراستی is 'wrongness / injustice' and ننگرد is the negative-present-form of نگرَ (ن + نگرد) 'does not look upon / does not regard' — together giving the sense 'should not err and should not countenance/tolerate injustice.' The classical negative prefix appears on ننگرد rather than repeated before بکژی in this construction.
segments.s105-g41: وگر (conditional) introduces a hypothetical: 'if'. آزمندیست is the noun آزمندی ('greed/desire') plus the archaic copula -ست, behaving like 'there is/it is greed'; the following nouns اندوه and رنج are coordinated by و and are understood as additional predicates or items in the same condition (the copula is not repeated). Word order is noun + -ست then coordinating conjunctions and nouns.
segments.s105-g42: شدن here is the infinitive/Verbal noun meaning 'to become / to come about' and combines with تنگ‌دل (an adjectival compound 'narrow‑hearted') to form تنگ‌دل شدن 'to become narrow‑hearted' (i.e., 'narrow‑heartedness arise'). در introduces the locative phrase; سرای سپنج is a noun phrase 'the house/shelter of سپنج' (سپنج literal 'sponge'), so در سرای سپنج gives the location where this becoming occurs. Note the verbal idea 'شدن' is placed before its noun complement in this poetic order.
segments.s105-g43: مگر functions as 'unless' (introducing the single exception/condition). رام گرد(د) is a passive/causative idea 'become tamed' — گردد is the classical present/subjunctive form 'becomes' or 'may become'. بر + ین form the demonstrative 'برین' ('on/this' → 'this') placed before the name کیقباد, so the phrase reads 'this Kay Qobad becomes tamed.' The poetic order puts the verb (گردد) between adjective and demonstrative.
segments.s105-g44: سر مرد بخرد should be read as a genitive grouping 'سرِ مردِ بخرد' — 'the head of the wise man' (بخرد functioning as adjective/noun 'wise'). نگردد is the negative present/subjunctive from گشت/گرد (ن- negative + گردد), 'will not be turned / does not turn.' ز is the ablative/causal 'from' and داد is 'justice'; together نگردد ز داد = 'does not (be) turned away from justice.' The verb comes at the end, typical of the classical/Persian clause.
segments.s105-g45: کس از ما here is a partitive/indefinite expression 'none/anyone of us' (in context rendered 'none of us'). نبینند is a negative present/subjunctive/optative plural form 'may they not see / shall not see' — in classical Persian verbs sometimes appear in plural with an indefinite subject like کس. جیحون بخواب is a noun + adverbial/participial phrase: جیحون 'the Jihun (river)' and بخواب 'in sleep / sleeping' (here used adverbially, not as an imperative).
segments.s105-g46: وز is functioning as a coordinating/prepositional particle here ('and/from'), a usage not reflected by the listed senses (see -1). ایران is the place 'Iran', and نیایند is the negative present/subjunctive/plural 'they do not come / shall not come' (again optative/negative). از + ین (ازین) is the classical compound 'from this', followed by روی آب 'the surface/side of the water' = 'this side of the river' — together 'from this side of the water.'
segments.s105-g47: مگر again means 'unless' (marking the single proviso). با is the preposition 'with' governing the coordinated list درود و سلام و پیام — 'greeting(s), salutation(s), and a message.' The nouns are simply coordinated after the preposition; this whole prepositional phrase supplies the means/condition by which what follows could happen.
segments.s105-g48: دو کشور شود is 'the two countries become / will be made' (شود = 'become' or passive 'are made'). زین سخن is a causal/ablative phrase 'by/from this speech/these words' (ز + این = 'from/with this'); the provided senses for زین don't fit, so treated as the classical 'از این' compound. شادكام is predicate adjective 'happy/fortunate'. The structure: 'دو کشور شود ... شادکام' = 'the two countries will be made happy by these words.'
segments.s105-g73: بنه is a noun placed before the verb for emphasis and here means a small encampment/tent (poetic use). برنهاد is an archaic past form of نهادن ('to set/put/raise'), so بنه برنهاد = 'he set up the camp.' و is the coordinating conjunction 'and.' سپه را uses the object marker را to mark سپه ('the host, army') as the definite object, and براند (from راندن) is a finite past verb 'drove/led' or 'marshaled' acting on سپه را.
segments.s105-g74: همی is a particle here marking continuous or ongoing action ('kept on / continually'). گرد is used as the noun 'dust' (not the preposition meaning 'around'). بر is the preposition 'upon/toward' and آسمان is 'the sky.' برفشاند is a transitive verb 'to scatter/throw upon' (here: to send dust into the air). Word order (particle + noun + preposition + object + verb) is poetically inverted but standard for the genre.
segments.s105-g75: ز is the poetic preposition 'from.' جیحون is the proper name (the Amu Darya). گذر here is the noun used in a light-verb construction گذر کردن, with کرد as the light verb: گذر کرد = 'he passed/crossed.' مانند باد is a simile 'like the wind' (باد = wind).
segments.s105-g76: وزان is a fused particle meaning 'and from that / from that (event).' آگهی is 'news/information' and شد is the past of شدن used here to mean 'came to pass' or 'occurred' (i.e., 'word came'). بر is the preposition 'to/toward' when combined with a person (بر کیقباد = 'to Kay Qobad'). کیقباد is the king's name.
segments.s105-g77: که introduces the content clause ('that ...'). دشمن شد is a simple past 'the enemy became/was (so)'; از پیش is a prepositional phrase meaning 'before/in front (of them)' or 'previously' depending on context; بی‌کارزار is a compound adjective 'without battle / without contest' describing the state of the enemy (i.e., rendered harmless or not engaging in combat).
segments.s105-g78: بدان here functions adverbially ('thereupon' / 'because of that'), formed from the prefix ب- + anaphoric element; گشت is the past of گشتن meaning 'became' or 'turned into.' شادان دل is an adjectival phrase 'joyful-hearted,' with شادان modifying دل (heart), and شهریار is the subject 'the king.' The clause thus reads 'thereupon the king's heart became joyful.'
segments.s105-g79: بدو is a prepositional phrase 'to him / unto him' (be + 3rd-person clitic) indicating the addressee of the speech. گفت here functions as the past tense verb 'said' (note: the provided lexical senses list is nominal, so sense mapping is marked -1). رستم is the speaker's name. که‌ای شهریار is direct address: که‌ای (an archaic vocative combining که + ای) = 'O' and شهریار = 'king' (vocative).
segments.s105-g80: مجو is the negative imperative ('do not seek'). آشتی is the noun 'reconciliation / truce.' درگه is 'court / threshold / place' (here 'the place' or 'seat'), and کارزار means 'the battlefield / field of battle.' The phrase directs: 'Do not seek reconciliation at the place of battle.'
segments.s105-g25: همان is a demonstrative meaning “that same”; بخش is the noun “portion/part.” ایرج is the personal name receiving the portion (indirect object placed before the verb). ز ایران‌زمین is a poetic genitive/partitive (‘from/of the land of Iran’) specifying the source of the portion.
segments.s105-g26: بداد here is an archaic/poetic past-form meaning ‘gave’ (the form with initial ب- is common in Ferdowsi); آفریدون is the subject (Fereydun). و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” کرد + آفرین is a light-verb construction: کرد ‘did/made’ + آفرین ‘praise/acclamation’ = ‘praised (him).’
segments.s105-g27: ازان‌گر is a conditional/concessive phrase (‘if from that’ / ‘if so’). بگردیم is 1st-person-plural subjunctive/present (“we turn/should turn”); the subjunctive here expresses a hypothetical action. و connects the clauses; جنگ آوریم is another subjunctive clause where آوریم (‘we bring/bring about’) takes جنگ as its object: ‘and we bring about war’ (i.e. wage war).
segments.s105-g28: جهان is the noun ‘world’; بر is the preposition ‘on/upon’; دل خویش is a possessive phrase ‘(our) own heart’ formed by دل ‘heart’ + خوی + ش (the clitic). تنگ آوریم is a causative/compound: تنگ ‘narrow’ + آوریم ‘we bring/cause’ = ‘we make (the) world narrow upon our hearts’ (i.e. cause distress).
segments.s105-g29: بود here functions as a copular/existential auxiliary giving a hypothetical result (‘there would be’). زخم شمشیر is a genitive-noun phrase ‘the wound of the sword.’ و links to خشم خدای ‘the wrath/anger of God.’ Together this lists two dire consequences.
segments.s105-g30: نیابیم is the negative subjunctive/optative ‘we would not find/obtain’ (ن- + یاب + یم). بهره means ‘share/portion.’ به هر دو سرای literally ‘in/to each/both abodes/realms’ — here implying both worlds (this world and the next).
segments.s105-g31: و‌گر is a conditional ‘if’/‘but if.’ همچنان ‘likewise/in the same way’ qualifies the comparison. چون + فریدون is ‘as Fereydun’ (comparing the action to Fereydun’s), and گرد here is a verbal/poetic element implying the action of turning/acting in that way (an archaic/poetic verbal form tied to the clause).
segments.s105-g32: به ... سپرد is the verb phrase ‘entrusted to …’. The repeated به is the preposition ‘to/for’ introducing each recipient: تور (Tūr), سلم (Salm) and ایرج (Iraj) are the three named recipients; سپرد is the past tense ‘entrusted/handed over.’ The list of names is coordinated by و.
segments.s105-g89: This is a simple prepositional span giving a origin–destination range: ز (poetic preposition “from”) + place name زاولستان, followed by تا “to/until” and the destination بدریای سند. The order is typical Persian: from X تا Y. Here بدریای and سند form a genitive-like unit (“the (sea/place) of Sind”).
segments.s105-g90: Main clause: نوشتیم (perfective ‘we wrote/inscribed’) is the finite verb; عهدی is the direct object ‘a covenant/promise’; ترا is split into ت (2nd-person clitic) + را (definite object marker) so ترا = ‘you’ as the object; بر پرند is a prepositional phrase ‘on/upon a bird’ (بر = on, پرند = bird). Word order is verb after object (typical older Persian/poetic), with the recipient encoded as clitic + object marker.
segments.s105-g91: Noun phrase functioning as the object/target of the following imperatives: سر تخت = “head (on) the throne” (سر + تخت, the noun + location), followed by با افسر نیمروز “with the helmet of Nimrūz” (با = with, افسر = helmet, نیمروز = the place/name Nimrūz). The preposition با links the instrument/attendant item to the head-on-throne phrase.
segments.s105-g92: Imperative clause: بدار is an imperative meaning ‘keep/hold/maintain’ (here used with the previously expressed throne/head), followed by coordinating و ‘and’, همی (an adverb meaning ‘ever/still’), باش imperative ‘be!’, and the predicate گیتی فروز ‘world-illuminating’ (گیتی = world, فروز = illuminating/bright). The two imperatives form a coupled command: ‘keep it and be ever world‑illuminating.’
segments.s105-g93: Imperative clause: وزین روی is a descriptive noun phrase ‘with that weighty/dignified face/mien’ (وزین = dignified, روی = face/countenance). کابل is the place/name ‘Kabul’ and به مهراب ده is the verb phrase ‘give to Mehrāb’ with به marking the indirect object and ده (imperative of دادن) meaning ‘give’. Word order places the descriptive prepositional/nominal phrase before the give-command.
segments.s105-g94: Parallel imperative: سراسر سنانت = ‘all/throughout your spear(s)’ (سراسر = throughout/all, سنانت = your spear(s)), به زهراب ده = ‘give to Zahrāb’ (به = to, زهراب = recipient name, ده = give imperative). The object is quantified by سراسر and the indirect object is marked with به.
segments.s105-g95: Rhetorical question/negation: کجا ‘where’ introduces the question; پادشاهیست is the abstract noun ‘kingship/sovereignty’ (predicate), followed by بی‌جنگ نیست literally ‘is not without war’ — i.e., there is no kingship free of war. The negative نیست applies to the phrase بی‌جنگ, yielding a rhetorical assertion that kingship without war does not exist.
segments.s105-g96: Contrastive conditional clause: وگر ‘but if’ + چند here ‘many/several’ (an indefinite quantifier) + روی زمین ‘on the face/surface of the earth’ (روی = surface, زمین = earth) + تنگ نیست ‘is not narrow’ (tightly negated). Together it expresses the thought ‘but if there were many, the earth would not be too cramped,’ i.e. even many rulers would not make the earth too small.
segments.s105-g105: یکی is an indefinite determiner meaning 'a certain / one'; جامه شهریاری is a noun + attributive noun ('robe of kingship' = 'royal robe') with the modifier شهریاری following جامه. به زر is a prepositional/colocational phrase ('in/with gold') that attaches to جامه; the whole segment is a nominal phrase (no finite verb here).
segments.s105-g106: ز is the poetic preposition 'of/from' marking material or ornament; یاقوت و پیروزه are coordinated materials ('ruby and turquoise') and تاج و کمر is the coordinated head noun ('crown and girdle/belt'). Word order is material → head noun (material phrase postmodifies the crown and belt).
segments.s105-g107: نهادند is the 3rd‑person past plural 'they placed/put'; مهد is the direct object 'cradle'. از بر (from/on top of) + پنج پیل expresses location 'on/above five elephants' with the numeral before the noun, a normal Persian order.
segments.s105-g108: ز marks the material/ornament ('with/of'); پیروزه رخشان is a noun + postpositive adjective ('turquoise, shining/radiant') and بکردار is an adjectival description ('of good/regular form' or 'well‑ornamented'); نیل here denotes the deep blue/indigo color. The whole phrase is a string of adjectival modifiers describing the cradle.
segments.s105-g109: بگسترد is past tense 'they spread/laid out'; زر is the material/object spread; بفت (classical past of بافتن) 'they wove' or 'they embroidered' — the two verbs coordinate and both take مهد ('upon the cradle') as location, here repeated with بر for emphasis ('upon the cradle, upon').
segments.s105-g110: یکی گنج کش is a nominal compound: یکی 'a certain/one' + گنج 'treasure' + کش as an agentive element forming 'treasure‑drawer/treasure‑finder'. کس ندانست مر means 'no one made (him) known to me' (کس = no one/someone; ندانست = did not know/ did not tell; مر = to me). The clause is elliptical: no one told me who that treasure‑finder was.
segments.s105-g111: فرستاد is past tense 'he sent'; نزدیک here means 'to the near/close (hands of)' and دستان سام is a genitive/possessive phrase 'the hands of Sam' (i.e. 'into Sam's hands' or 'to Sam himself'). The verb precedes the prepositional phrase, a normal order in older Persian.
segments.s105-g112: که introduces a subordinate clause giving the reason ('for/because'); خلعت is 'the robe/robe of honour' and م + را together function as the clitic object 'mara' = 'to me' (here split in the word list). زین 'from/by this' + فزون 'in excess/more' + بود (past 'was') + کام 'desire/satisfaction' form a predicative phrase: 'the robe, for me, was more than this in satisfying/desire' — i.e. 'the robe more than fulfilled my desire.' Note: م and را are written separately in the word list but form the object clitic مرا.
segments.s105-g113: اگر opens a conditional clause ('if'). باشدم is an archaic/poetic first‑person subjunctive/optative form meaning 'may I be' / 'if I live' and is followed by the noun phrase زندگانی دراز ('a long life') which is the complement of the verb; literal order is If (اگر) + (may) I be (باشدم) + life (زندگانی) long (دراز).
segments.s105-g114: ترا is the object 'you' (here written as ت + را), where ت is the enclitic 2nd‑person object and را is the object/definite marker attached; دارم is the present 'I have/possess'. اندر جهان is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the world', and بی‌نیاز is a predicative adjective 'without need/self‑sufficient' describing the speaker's state as a result of possessing 'you'.
segments.s105-g115: همان is a demonstrative 'the same / that very' modifying the following names; the sequence قارن نیو و کشواد constitutes a coordinated noun phrase of personal names, and the final را is the object marker attached to the whole phrase (poetic listing of recipients).
segments.s105-g116: چو here functions as 'as / like' (introducing a parallel list). برزین is a personal name, و links the two names, خراد appears as part of a compound name with پولاد (Kharad‑Pulād) and پولاد is used as a male name; the final را again marks the phrase as an object — the clause parallels the previous list.
segments.s105-g117: برافگند is a past third‑person verb ('he cast/put on/placed') built with the prefix بر‑ + افگند (poetic past). خلعت is the direct object 'the robe (of honour)'. چنان چون سزید is a parenthetical adverbial phrase 'thus, as was fitting' where سزید (from سزا) is a past form meaning 'befitted / deserved' (it expresses 'as was due').
segments.s105-g118: کسی را ('someone' + object marker را) starts a relative construction: که introduces the relative clause. خلعت سزاوار دید is 'he saw (دید) [him] deserving (سزاوار) of the robe (خلعت)' — syntactically, 'whom he perceived as worthy of the robe.' Note the implicit subject of دید ('he') carried over from the narrative.
segments.s105-g119: This is a list of objects given: درم (dirham) followed by the verb داد ('gave') and then more items (دینار, تیغ, سپر). In poetic word order the verb appears after the first item but governs the whole coordinated list: 'he gave dirhams, dinars, a sword, and a shield.'
segments.s105-g120: کرا is an archaic/formal relative/indefinite 'to whom / whoever'. در خور آمد is a fixed phrase meaning 'it came in his share / it was fitting' (در + خور + آمد); here آمد is the verb 'came', and کلاه و کمر ('cap and belt') are the items that 'came' to those people — the phrase shows a poetic inversion (the person marked by کرا is fronted while the things given follow the verb).
segments.s105-g57: بدین روزگار اندر افراسیاب is an adverbial time + locative phrase. بدین روزگار (literally 'in this time(s)') is a poetic time‑phrase placed first; اندر is an archaic preposition meaning 'in/among' that precedes the proper name افراسیاب, so the whole segment sets the scene 'in these days, Afrasiab...'.
segments.s105-g58: بیامد is a simple past, third‑person singular ('he came'). به تیزی is an adverbial phrase formed with به + noun meaning 'with speed/quickly' (here به = by/‑ly). و coordinates to the next clause; بگذاشت is a past transitive verb with the poetic/prefixal ب‑, 'he let/released/caused to set', and آب is its direct object placed after the verb.
segments.s105-g59: شنیدی is the perfect/past 'have you heard' (second person singular or general narrative 'you have heard'). که introduces the subordinate content clause; با شاه نوذر is a prepositional phrase 'with/to King Nowzar' marking the target/affected party; چه کرد is an indirect interrogative construction ('what he did'), with چه (what) placed before the verb کرد as is normal in Persian questions.
segments.s105-g60: This clause uses شد (became) as the main copula ('became'). دل دام و دد is a coordinated noun phrase ('heart, snare, and enemy') serving as the subject; پر از is the adjectival/complement phrase 'full of' followed by the nouns داغ و درد ('burning grief and pain') which are the contents filling the subject.
segments.s105-g61: ز is the poetic preposition 'from / because of', marking cause ('out of hatred'). کینه is the causal noun 'hatred'. به introduces the target (اغریرث, a poetic/place/name) and پرخرد is a post‑nominal compound adjective meaning 'broken into very small pieces / very small' here describing the state ('filled or made small/shattered').
segments.s105-g62: نه is sentential negation 'not.' آن کرد = 'he did that/so' (آن = that; کرد = did). کز is a relative/subordinating particle 'which/that from', connecting to the following phrase. مردمی در خورد uses مردمی 'of the people/common' and خورد in the sense 'befall/ happen to' (here 'what befalls or is usual among the people'), so the clause says 'not what befalls/what people experience' → 'he did not act as ordinary people do.'
segments.s105-g63: ز marks cause or provenance ('because of / from') and is followed by کردار ('deed, action') with the modifier بد‌گر (an adjective/agentive compound meaning 'evil/wicked'). پشیمان شوید is an exhortation 'become regretful' — شوید is the present/subjunctive/imperative plural form of شدن used here as an exhortative 'be (you) ...'.
segments.s105-g64: بنوی is an imperative 'write!' or 'record!'; it can introduce an imperative or a directive clause. ز سر باز is an idiomatic sequence where ز = 'from', سر = 'head/lead' (here idiomatically 'from the outset' or 'from the bond/head'), and باز = 'again/free' (here 'free' or 'anew'); پیمان شوید uses شوید ('become') with پیمان ('pledge/covenant') to mean 'become pledged' or in this context 'be freed from the pledge' depending on the idiomatic reading — taken together the clause issues the record/command about being freed or renewed in relation to the covenant.
segments.s105-g97: سرش را (sar+‑e‑sh + object marker) = 'his head' marked as definite object by را; بیاراست is a poetic past (3rd sg.) of بیاراستن 'to adorn/array'. با تاج زر is an instrumental phrase 'with a crown of gold' modifying the verb. Word order is object (سرش را) + verb + instrument (با ...) typical of Persian poetic inversion.
segments.s105-g98: همان here functions as 'likewise/that same' linking this clause to the previous action. گردگاهش (گردگاه + ‑ش) = 'his neck/collar area'. به زرین کمر is a descriptive/locative phrase literally 'to/with a golden belt/girdle' (زرین = golden, کمر = belt/waist). The clause means he set/arrayed his neck with a golden girdle; the verb is carried over from the previous clause by coordination.
segments.s105-g99: ز یک روی گیتی is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'from one side/face of the world' (ز = 'from', روی = 'side/face', گیتی = 'world'). مرو را سپرد is a past (3rd sg.) clause 'he entrusted/sent Merv' where را marks مرو as definite object and سپرد is the past of سپردن 'to entrust/hand over'. Poetic economy leaves the subject implicit (carried from prior lines).
segments.s105-g100: ببوسید is past (3rd sg.) 'he kissed'. روی زمین is a locative phrase 'upon the ground' (روی = 'on/upon', زمین = 'ground/earth'). مرد گرد is a compact, somewhat archaic phrase: مرد = 'man' and گرد here functions as a verbal/participial sense 'to go about / turn', so the sense is roughly 'the man went about/turned' — syntactically the clauses are coordinated actions (he kissed the ground; the man moved/was about).
segments.s105-g101: ازان پس (= از آن پس) is a temporal adverbial 'after that / thereafter'. چنین گفت is 'thus said'; in Persian reported speech the verb گفت 'said' follows the adverbial. فرخ قباد is a proper name (the speaker). The whole clause is a standard narrative formula 'Thereafter Farrokh Qobad said thus.'
segments.s105-g102: که introduces the content of his speech ('that...'). بی‌زال is an epithet 'without Zal' (بی‑ = without, Zal is a name), modifying تخت بزرگی 'a great throne/seat' (تخت = throne, بزرگی = greatness/large). مباد is an archaic optative/negative 'may it not be' (a prayer/wish that such a thing not happen). Together: 'that there may be no great throne without Zal.'
segments.s105-g103: به یک موی is a standard concessive/measure phrase 'for one hair' (به = 'for/to', یک موی = 'a single hair'). دستان here functions as a proper-name-like reference (contextually a valued person or thing) and is the possessor/measure head: 'a single hair of Dastān'. نیرزد is the negative present/past sense 'is not worth / does not merit'. جهان = 'the world' is the thing judged not worth that hair. The construction is idiomatic: 'the world is not worth a single hair of X.'
segments.s105-g104: که introduces explanatory subordinate clause 'that...'. او = 'he/that one' (referring back to the person named). ماندمان here is a noun-like form meaning 'the remnant/what remained for us' (poetic formation); یادگار = 'memorial/memento'. از مهان = 'from the guests/strangers' (از = from/of, مهان = guests). Together: 'that he remained for us as a memento from the guests.'
segments.s105-g17: بران هم که کرد آفریدون نخست is a compact introductory clause: بران (an adverb, 'thereupon/then') + هم ('also') + که (conjunction introducing the content clause) + کرد (past tense 'did') + آفریدون (subject 'Afaridun') + نخست ('first', adverb). Word order is topical/adverbial then verb then subject; 'که' here links this clause to what Afaridun did (the following clause).
segments.s105-g18: کجا راستی را به بخشش بجست is the content clause introduced by the preceding 'که'. کجا 'where' begins the interrogative/indirect question; راستی را ('rightness/justice' + object marker را) is the direct object of بجست ('sought' — past tense). به بخشش is a prepositional phrase 'for/by a grant' specifying the manner/purpose. The verb بجست is an older past form meaning 'sought'.
segments.s105-g19: سزد‌گر برانیم دل هم بران: سزد‌گر is an impersonal/factive adjective 'fitting/appropriate' functioning as a comment ('it is fitting'). برانیم is a 1st-person-plural optative/subjunctive-like form from راندن 'to send/drive' meaning 'we should send/drive (there)'; دل 'heart' is the direct object; هم 'also' adds emphasis; بران here is adverbial 'thither/thereupon'. The clause uses an older subjunctive/optative flavor common in epic Persian.
segments.s105-g20: نگردیم از آیین و راه سران: نگردیم is negative present/subjunctive/optative 'may we not turn / we will not turn' (here negated volitive sense). از marks separation 'from'; آیین 'custom/rite' and راه 'way' are coordinated by و; سران 'the chiefs/leaders' qualifies whose rites/way these are. The verb precedes the prepositional complement, a normal poetic word order.
segments.s105-g21: ز جیحون و تا ماورالنهر بر is a range expression: ز ... تا 'from ... to/as far as' with جیحون ('the Jeyhun/River Amu Darya') and ماورالنهر ('Ma wara' al-nahr, lands beyond the river') marking endpoints. بر after the phrase is an adverb/preposition used poetically to mark the area 'over/around'. The preposition ز (poetic form of از) begins the range.
segments.s105-g22: که جیحون میانچیست اندر گذر is an explanatory clause introduced by که ('for/that'). میانچیست is a poetic fused expression ('میان' + 'چیست') meaning literally 'what lies between' or 'what runs between' and is used here in present-tense rhetorical sense '(which) runs between/through'. اندر گذر ('in/within the pass') is a locative phrase; word order places the predicate (میانچیست) before the locative, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s105-g23: بر و بوم ما بود هنگام شاه: بر و بوم ('land, realm') is a fixed collocation meaning 'domain'; ما is the possessive 'our'; بود is the past copula 'was' (past tense); هنگام شاه is a temporal phrase 'at the time of the king'. The noun phrase precedes the verb ('was') in typical Persian order here because of the poetic inversion.
segments.s105-g24: نکردی بران مرز ایرج نگاه: نکردی is the past negative of کردن 'did not do' (here functioning as 'did not cast' in combination with نگاه). بران is the adverb 'to/at that place'; مرز ایرج is a genitival noun phrase 'the border of Irāj'; نگاه ('gaze/view') combines with کردن to form 'to look' or 'to cast a glance'. Word order puts the verb before its object and then the subject/agent (ایرج) — a normal poetic ordering.
segments.s105-g9: وزو is an archaic/clitic form (literally وز او) not meaning ablution here; it functions as a fronting/connecting particle referring back to Fereydun. بر روان فریدون درود is a fixed felicitation formula: بر + noun (روان) + proper name (فریدون) followed by درود (a greeting/blessing). Word order places the preposition and phrase before the blessing noun.
segments.s105-g10: کزو is a relative/adverbial 'from whom' placed before the verb for emphasis. دارد is present-tense 'has/possesses' (here used as the main verb). این تخم ما is the subject ('this seed of ours') following the verb (classical Persian allows verb before subject), and تار و پود is a predicate nominal (a woven-metaphor: 'warp and weft').
segments.s105-g11: گر is the conditional 'if'. از تور marks the source/agent ('through/from Tur'), and بر ایرج indicates the direction/target ('upon/against Irāj'); the epithet نیک‌بخت follows the name as an appositive. The prepositional phrases appear before the rest of the clause as is common in classical usage.
segments.s105-g12: بد آمد پدید is a compact poetic clause meaning 'an evil/misfortune came into being' — آمد is the past tense verb 'came' (or 'befell') and پدید is a postpositive complement 'appeared/emerged'. از پی here functions as 'in pursuit of' or 'on account of the quest for', and تاج و تخت is a compound object 'crown and throne' (sovereignty).
segments.s105-g13: بران is an adverbial/connecting particle ('thereupon/for that reason'). بر همی راند is an idiomatic verb phrase: بر هم = 'against one another' and راند (past of راندن) 'drove/caused to fight', with همی marking continuous/aspectual flavor. باید سخن places the modal 'it is necessary' before the noun 'saying/tale' — classical Persian often uses باید + noun where English would use 'must speak'.
segments.s105-g14: بباید is an alternative/emphatic form of باید ('must/it ought to be'); که introduces the content clause. پیوند is the subject/object 'the bond/link', ماند is the present/subjunctive-like 'remain/last' (here a desiderative/result clause), and به بن ('to the root') marks the locus where the bond should endure. Word order places the noun before the verb, typical in this style.
segments.s105-g15: گر introduces the conditional 'if'. این کینه از ایرج آمد پدید literally 'this grudge came forth from Irāj' — آمد is the past verb 'came/happened' and پدید acts as a complement meaning 'became manifest/appeared'. The phrase states the hypothetical origin of the hatred.
segments.s105-g16: منوچهر is the subject. سرتاسر ('throughout/in full') precedes the object آن کین ('that vengeance/hatred') to stress extent; کشید is the past of کشیدن used idiomatically with کین to mean 'took up/exacted (vengeance)'. The verb follows the object (SOV order) as is common in older Persian.
segments.s105-g49: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning 'when'. نامه is the direct object 'the letter'. به مهر اندر آورد is a verbal phrase: به مهر ('with a seal') + اندر آورد (past tense 'brought/put in') = 'sealed' or 'put a seal on'. شاه is the subject placed after the verb for poetic inversion (verb before subject).
segments.s105-g50: فرستاد is the main past verb 'sent/dispatch(ed)'. نزدیک ایران is an adverbial phrase 'near/toward Iran' modifying the sending, and سپاه is the direct object 'the army'; the order (verb + adverbial + object) reflects poetic inversion from ordinary SOV.
segments.s105-g51: ببردند is a past-tense third-person plural 'they brought/took'. نامه‌بر is the object 'the messenger / letter‑carrier'. کیقباد is the recipient/name placed without a preposition (a common poetic omission) meaning 'to Kay Qobad'.
segments.s105-g52: سخن is 'the speech / message' or topic. نیز is the adverb 'also/likewise'. ازین گونه is a frozen phrase 'in this way / of this kind' (از + این with گونه 'manner'). کردند is the past plural 'they did/made', and یاد here with کردند forms 'کردند یاد' = 'they mentioned / recounted / made mention'. Word order places the manner phrase before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s105-g53: چنین داد پاسخ is 'he gave such an answer' (چنین 'thus/so' + داد 'gave' + پاسخ 'answer'). که introduces reported content ('that'). دانی is the archaic/polite second-person present 'you know' or imperative-stative 'know' used in reported speech, and درست functions as an adverb 'accurately/truly'.
segments.s105-g54: This clause is the content of the reply: که 'that' + از ما 'from us' (indicating source/agent) + نبد (classical negative 'was not / is not') + پیشدستی 'precedence / taking the initiative' + نخست 'first'. Together: 'that it is not (from us) to take the initiative first' = 'we do not take the initiative first.'
segments.s105-g55: ز (poetic 'from') indicates source. تور is the proper name 'Tur' (ancestor of the Turanians). اندر آمد is a compound past 'came/occurred'. نخستین ستم is a noun phrase 'the first oppression/injustice' (نخستین qualifies ستم). The verb precedes the noun phrase here due to poetic word order.
segments.s105-g56: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause: شاهی 'kingship / share of rule' + چو 'when' + ایرج 'Iraj' + شد 'became/was' + از تخت کم literally 'small from the throne' — an idiom meaning 'had only a small share of the throne' or 'his portion of the kingship was small'. Word order and the adjective phrase (از تخت کم) are typical of classical/poetic Persian.

sentences.s105-s1: The commander of the Turks, his two eyes full of tears, was struck with astonishment by Afrasiab.
sentences.s105-s2: He chose a certain clever man and sent him as an envoy to Iran, as was fitting.
sentences.s105-s3: He wrote a letter in the style of Artang, having adorned it with a hundred kinds of colors and designs.
sentences.s105-s4: In the name of the Lord of the Sun and the Moon, who gave order to creation.
sentences.s105-s33: I have no hatred or greed; I have suffered much in the house of sponge.
sentences.s105-s34: I set you upon that water — could Afrasiab find peace?
sentences.s105-s35: Write it down: once again someone wrote a covenant, and he planted a tree in a great garden.
sentences.s105-s36: A messenger came like a leopard; he delivered the letter to Peshang's presence.
sentences.s105-s17: Let us forgive, and thereafter not seek vengeance, for many calamities are not worth the earth itself.
sentences.s105-s18: The poet, by years, turned like snow; from the blood of the Kians the earth became bright/red (shangarf).
sentences.s105-s19: In the end, nobody gains any share from his place except what belongs to his own rank.
sentences.s105-s20: On the last day we shall remain beneath the earth, entirely wrapped in coarse cloth and in the place of the abyss.
sentences.s105-s41: There had been no earlier sign of truce; on this day my mace brought them.
sentences.s105-s42: Thus spoke the renowned Kay Qobad: I have seen nothing better than justice.
sentences.s105-s43: The scion of Fereydun, Farrokh Pashang, will, for a while, turn his head away from battle.
sentences.s105-s44: It befits anyone who has wisdom not to err and not to tolerate injustice.
sentences.s105-s21: And if there is greed and sorrow and suffering — and narrow‑heartedness comes to be in the sponge‑house.
sentences.s105-s22: Unless this Kay Qobad becomes tamed, the head of a wise man will not turn away from justice.
sentences.s105-s23: None of us shall see the Jihun asleep, nor shall they come from Iran to this side of the water.
sentences.s105-s24: Unless, with greetings and salutations and a message, the two countries are made happy by these words.
sentences.s105-s37: He pitched (his) camp and marshaled the host, continually flinging dust up toward the sky.
sentences.s105-s38: He crossed the Jeyhun like the wind, and from that news reached Kay Qobad.
sentences.s105-s39: That the enemy, before them, was left without a fight — thereupon the king's heart became joyful.
sentences.s105-s40: Rostam said to him, 'O king, do not seek reconciliation in the place of battle.'
sentences.s105-s13: Fereydun gave that same portion of the land of Iran to Iraj and praised him.
sentences.s105-s14: But if, from that, we turn away and make war,
sentences.s105-s15: There would be the wound of the sword and the wrath of God; we would not obtain a share in both abodes.
sentences.s105-s16: And if likewise, as Fereydun did, he had distributed [it], he entrusted it to Tur, to Salm, and to Iraj.
sentences.s105-s45: From Zābulistān to the sea of Sind I wrote a covenant to you upon a bird.
sentences.s105-s46: Place your head on the throne with the helmet of Nimrūz; keep it, and so be ever the world's light.
sentences.s105-s47: Give Kabul to Mehrāb with that stately countenance; give all your spears to Zahrāb.
sentences.s105-s48: Where is there a kingship that is without war? But if there were many, the earth would not be too narrow.
sentences.s105-s53: They placed a royal robe of gold, and a crown and girdle of ruby and turquoise.
sentences.s105-s54: They placed the cradle upon five elephants, radiant with turquoise and with indigo ornamentation.
sentences.s105-s55: They spread gold and wove it upon the cradle; a certain treasure‑puller — no one made him known to me.
sentences.s105-s56: He sent (them) into Sam’s own hands, for the robe more than fulfilled my desire.
sentences.s105-s57: If I live a long life, I will have you and be free of need in the world.
sentences.s105-s58: The same Qaran, Niu and Keshvad — and likewise Barzin and Kharad‑Pulād.
sentences.s105-s59: He cast the robe thus, as was fitting, upon anyone whom he saw deserving of it.
sentences.s105-s60: He gave dirhams and dinars and sword and shield; to whomever it was fitting came a cap and a belt.
sentences.s105-s29: At that time Afrasiab came swiftly and released the waters.
sentences.s105-s30: Have you heard what he did to King Nowzar? The heart — snare and foe — became full of burning grief and pain.
sentences.s105-s31: Out of hatred he filled Aghrīrath with fragments; he did not do what befalls common people.
sentences.s105-s32: Be remorseful for evil deeds; write (it down): be freed from the pledge again.
sentences.s105-s49: He adorned his head with a golden crown, and likewise his neck with a golden girdle.
sentences.s105-s50: From one side of the world he sent (entrusted) Merv; he kissed the ground and the man went about.
sentences.s105-s51: Thereafter Farrokh Qobad thus spoke: 'May there be no great throne without Zal.'
sentences.s105-s52: The world is not worth a single hair of Dastān; he remains a memorial for us from the guests.
sentences.s105-s9: Thereupon Afṛīdūn too first sought where to make a rightful grant.
sentences.s105-s10: It was fitting that we should there send our hearts as well; we will not turn away from the rites and the way of the chiefs.
sentences.s105-s11: From the Jeyhun and as far as Ma wara' al-nahr — for the Jeyhun runs through the pass.
sentences.s105-s12: That territory was part of our realm in the king's time; Irāj did not cast his gaze toward that border.
sentences.s105-s5: Blessings upon the soul of Fereydun; from him this seed of ours has its warp and weft.
sentences.s105-s6: If, through Tur, misfortune befell the fortunate Irāj, it sprang from the pursuit of crown and throne.
sentences.s105-s7: Because he drove them against one another, it is necessary to speak; it must be that the bond remain at the root.
sentences.s105-s8: If this grudge arose from Irāj, Manuchehr exacted that vengeance in full.
sentences.s105-s25: When the king sealed the letter, he sent an army toward Iran.
sentences.s105-s26: They brought the messenger to Kay Qobad; they likewise recounted the matter in this manner.
sentences.s105-s27: He gave this reply: 'Know truly that we do not take the initiative first.'
sentences.s105-s28: From Tur came the first injustice, which arose when Iraj's share of the throne was small.
